questions.question: What is the main problem with naive syndrome measurement?

options.text: A single ancilla error can propagate to multiple data qubits

questions.explanation: In naive syndrome measurement, a single ancilla controls CNOTs to multiple data qubits. An X error on the ancilla propagates through each CNOT, causing errors on many data qubits—potentially exceeding the code's correction capacity.

questions.question: How does Shor-style syndrome extraction achieve fault tolerance?

options.text: By using separate ancilla qubits for each CNOT

questions.explanation: Shor-style uses separate ancilla qubits for each CNOT in the syndrome circuit. An error on ancilla i only affects data qubit i, ensuring a single fault causes at most one data error, maintaining fault tolerance.

questions.question: What is a cat state used for in fault-tolerant measurement?

options.text: As a verified ancilla for syndrome measurement

questions.explanation: Cat states (|000...0⟩ + |111...1⟩)/√2 are used as verified ancillas. They can be checked for errors before use, and errors on the cat state affect data qubits in a controlled way that maintains fault tolerance.

questions.question: Why are syndrome measurements repeated d times for a distance-d code?

options.text: To distinguish persistent data errors from transient measurement errors

questions.explanation: Repeating d times allows distinguishing real data errors (syndromes persist across rounds) from measurement errors (syndromes appear and disappear). This creates a 3D syndrome structure for accurate decoding.

questions.question: In the 3D syndrome picture, how do measurement errors appear?

options.text: As pairs of defects at different spatial locations at the same time
As pairs of defects at the same location at different times
As single defects that never pair
As defects that span the entire code

questions.explanation: Measurement errors appear as temporal defect pairs—a syndrome that's wrong in one round but correct before and after. In 3D, this looks like a defect pair separated in time but at the same spatial location.

questions.question: In destructive logical Z measurement, how is the logical outcome determined?

options.text: By measuring only the first qubit
By computing the parity of measurements along the Z_L support

questions.explanation: Destructive Z_L measurement involves measuring all data qubits in the Z basis, then computing the parity (XOR) of outcomes for qubits in the Z_L logical operator's support. This parity gives the Z_L eigenvalue.

questions.question: Cat state verification can detect all types of errors (both X and Z) that occurred during cat state preparation.
questions.explanation: False. Standard cat state verification (parity checks) detects X errors (bit flips) but not Z errors (phase flips). However, Z errors on the cat state don't propagate badly during syndrome measurement, so this is acceptable for fault tolerance.
